FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9429, ages 11-12

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization and perpetration only; B3 applies (cyberbullying alone NOT qualifying). Abstract states total screen time associated with cyberbullying, but no separately-measured SM with own coefficient reported for cyberbullying as outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple logistic regression; cyberbullying as dependent variable, sociodemographic predictors; Table 2 reports AOR with 95% CI

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2: AOR (adjusted odds ratios) with 95% CI for sociodemographic associations; Table 3: OR and 95% CI for screen time modalities

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis of cyberbullying prevalence and correlates

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article; no indication of duplicate/preprint

FT_EC7_reason: Cyberbullying is NOT SM as IV predicting external outcome; it is the dependent variable (cyberbullying victimization and perpetration). FT-EC7 only applies when SM is the DV. Additionally, analyses include SM exposure (internet, social media) but not as demographic-only predictors.
notes: Paper focuses on cyberbullying epidemiology with sociodemographic and screen time correlates. SM/internet mentioned as associated with cyberbullying but paper does not separately measure and report SM as an independent qualifying exposure variable with its own coefficient for the cyberbullying outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study baseline (2016-2018), N=10,755, ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Social networking (texting, video chat, social networking platforms), videos (YouTube), video games separately measured; B1 applies (multiple separate coefficients for contemporary screen modalities). Multiple linear regression with separate B coefficients for each modality.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses estimating associations between sociodemographic factors (IV) and screen time (DV); Tables III and IV report B coefficients with 95% CI

FT_IC4.evidence: Tables III and IV: B coefficients with 95% CI for all screen time modalities and sociodemographic associations

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis of contemporary screen time correlates

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article; no indication of duplicate/preprint

FT_EC7_reason: All THREE FT-EC7 conditions met: (a) Screen time (SM/smartphone) is DEPENDENT variable in all analyses; NO analyses with SM as IV predicting external outcome. (b) All predictors are demographic-only: sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education. (c) Cross-sectional analysis only (no longitudinal predictor-outcome analysis stated). B15 applies: this is cross-sectional sociodemographic→screen time, which is INCLUDE per instructions. FT-EC7 DOES apply.
notes: Purely descriptive analysis of screen time disparities by sociodemographic groups. All predictors are demographic-only (no sleep, mental health, parenting, etc.). All analyses cross-sectional. Per B15, cross-sectional sociodemographic→SM analyses are INCLUDED and FT-EC7 applies.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, December 2020 COVID-19 survey, N=4,106, ages 12-14

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media (COVID-related checking on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat) and television media measured as exposures with separate coefficients in regression models. B1 applies (separate coefficients for distinct modalities).

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression with coefficients transformed to risk ratios (RR); Table 2 reports RR and 95% CI for associations between information sources and masking behavior

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2: RR (risk ratios) and ARR (adjusted risk ratios) with 95% CI for all information sources and masking behaviors

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis of COVID-19 information sources and adolescent masking behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article; no indication of duplicate/preprint

FT_EC7_reason: Masking behavior is dependent variable (outcome), not SM. SM (social media) is an independent variable/exposure predicting masking behavior. FT-EC7 only applies when SM/smartphone is the DV; here it is IV. Additionally, non-demographic predictors included: parental counseling (B17 applies—parenting not in demographic-only set).
notes: Analysis of COVID-19 information sources (including social media exposure) on adolescent masking behaviors. Parental counseling is included as a key predictor, which disqualifies FT-EC7 (not demographic-only).
